_meta.purpose: Per-state insulation data dictionary for Phase A.2 state-vertical hub pages (attic R-value upgrade focus)
_meta.sources: IECC 2021 Table R402.1.3 prescriptive R-value mandatory minimums; state energy code adoption status (Building Codes Assistance Project tracker); DOE Better Buildings utility rebate registry; IRA HOMES + HEER program state allocations; state contractor licensing boards; BLS OEWS May 2024 (SOC 47-2131 Insulation Workers, Floor/Ceiling/Wall + 47-2132 Insulation Workers, Mechanical state means); NOAA NCEI 30-year (1991-2020) state-average heating + cooling degree days.

fields.iecc_zone: 2021 IECC climate zone (1-8, A=moist, B=dry, C=marine)
fields.required_attic_r_value: IECC 2021 prescriptive R-value mandatory minimum for vented attic (state-typical)
fields.required_wall_r_value: IECC 2021 prescriptive R-value mandatory minimum for above-grade wood-frame wall (state-typical)
fields.energy_code_basis: iecc-2021 / iecc-2018 / iecc-2015 / iecc-2012 / state-specific / no-statewide-code — adopted residential energy code basis
fields.stretch_code_available: yes / no — does state offer or mandate a stretch code beyond IECC baseline
fields.hdd_annual: NOAA NCEI 30-year state-average annual heating degree days (base 65°F)
fields.cdd_annual: NOAA NCEI 30-year state-average annual cooling degree days (base 65°F)
fields.vapor_barrier_required: warm-side / no-statewide-rule / climate-specific — vapor retarder mandate by climate zone
fields.dominant_attic_insulation: blown-cellulose / fiberglass-batt / blown-fiberglass / spray-foam-closed-cell / spray-foam-open-cell — predominant residential attic install material
fields.ira_rebate_program_status: live / launching / planning / not-yet — IRA HOMES + HEER state program implementation status
fields.license_board_name: Issuing authority for residential insulation contractor licensing
fields.permit_typical: Typical permit threshold for residential attic insulation upgrade
fields.insulation_worker_wage_mean_hourly: BLS OEWS May 2024 state mean hourly wage for SOC 47-2131 Insulation Workers, Floor/Ceiling/Wall (USD)
fields.avg_attic_upgrade_low: Typical low end of 1,500 sq ft attic upgrade R-30 → R-49 blown cellulose (USD)
fields.avg_attic_upgrade_high: Typical high end (2,500 sq ft attic + closed-cell spray foam roofline + air-sealing) (USD)
fields.distinctive_law: Single most distinctive state-specific energy code, insulation, or air-sealing rule
fields.climate_concern: Primary climate or code driver of insulation scope and material selection in this state

AL.name: Alabama
AL.permit_typical: no permit required for attic insulation retrofit; HBLB residential license required above $10,000 contract
AL.distinctive_law: Alabama Code §41-9-160 makes the Alabama Energy and Residential Codes Board the slowest-adopting state energy code authority in the Southeast — Alabama remains on the IECC 2015 cycle while neighboring Florida and Georgia have moved to IECC 2021, and the AERCB explicitly preempts every municipal energy code amendment, leaving Birmingham, Huntsville, and Mobile homeowners with no statewide path to require above-baseline insulation R-value on new construction or major retrofit.
AL.climate_concern: Alabama's combination of hot-humid Gulf Coast climate from Mobile to the Tennessee Valley creates a cooling-dominant insulation profile statewide — the 2,200 annual cooling degree days plus persistent summer humidity drive radiant-barrier-plus-blown-fiberglass attic systems on every retrofit, with Mobile-Baldwin coastal humidity additionally requiring closed-cell spray foam at the roofline on every Gulf Coast home addressing humidity-driven attic moisture cycling.

AK.name: Alaska
AK.license_board_name: Alaska Department of Commerce — Construction Contractors Section
AK.permit_typical: borough building permit required for attic insulation retrofit on most Alaska boroughs; Construction Contractor with Residential Endorsement required
AK.distinctive_law: Alaska Statute 18.65.082 and the Alaska Housing Finance Corporation BEES rating system create the most aggressive state energy code in the country — the Alaska BEES (Building Energy Efficiency Standard) requires above-IECC R-60 attic and R-21 wall insulation, plus mandatory blower-door testing on every new residential construction, with AHFC additionally administering the most generous state-funded weatherization program in the country covering 100% of attic and wall insulation upgrades for income-eligible Alaska households.
AK.climate_concern: Alaska's combination of 13,800 annual heating degree days, -50°F winter low temperatures across interior climate zones 7-8, and short summer cool-night temperatures forces every Alaska residential insulation retrofit toward maximum R-value and aggressive air-sealing — closed-cell spray foam at the roofline plus R-60+ blown attic insulation are now the dominant Alaska retrofit specs, with Bethel and Yukon-Kuskokwim Delta off-grid homes additionally requiring polyiso continuous exterior insulation and triple-pane window upgrades to manage heating fuel costs above $8/gallon delivered.

AZ.name: Arizona
AZ.license_board_name: Arizona Registrar of Contractors — license CR-39 (Insulation)
AZ.permit_typical: no permit required for attic insulation retrofit on existing residential; ROC CR-39 specialty license required on contracts above $1,000
AZ.distinctive_law: Arizona Registrar of Contractors §32-1121 sets one of the lowest residential contracting thresholds in the country at $1,000 — every Arizona insulation contractor exceeding that figure must hold a CR-39 Insulation specialty license, and Arizona was one of the first Mountain West states to launch the IRA HOMES + HEER weatherization rebate programs through the Arizona Governor's Office of Resiliency, with Tucson Electric Power and SRP additionally co-funding utility insulation rebates on every Arizona electric-heat-pump home retrofit.
AZ.climate_concern: Arizona's combination of 4,200 annual cooling degree days in the Phoenix and Tucson basins, extreme summer attic temperatures exceeding 150°F under tile and asphalt-shingle roofing, and minimal heating load drives a cooling-dominant insulation profile statewide — radiant-barrier sheathing plus R-49 blown-cellulose attic systems are now the dominant Arizona retrofit specs, with Flagstaff and high-elevation Northern Arizona requiring closed-cell spray foam at the roofline to manage the short but intense winter heating season.

AR.name: Arkansas
AR.license_board_name: Arkansas Contractors Licensing Board — Residential Remodeler (>$2,000)
AR.permit_typical: no permit required for attic insulation retrofit; residential remodeler license required above $2,000 contract
AR.distinctive_law: Arkansas Code §17-25-101 sets one of the lowest residential contracting thresholds at $2,000 — and Arkansas remains on the 2009 IECC for residential energy code, the oldest energy code basis of any U.S. state, leaving Little Rock, Fort Smith, and Fayetteville homeowners with the lowest prescriptive R-value floor in the Southeast and forcing voluntary above-code insulation upgrades through utility-incentive Energy Efficiency Arkansas programs rather than statewide code mandates.
AR.climate_concern: Arkansas's mixed-humid climate from the Ozark Mountains to the Mississippi Delta creates a cooling-leaning insulation profile with 3,700 heating and 1,800 cooling degree days — and Arkansas's high tree canopy plus pine-pollen-laden summer air requires careful attic ventilation balance with insulation upgrades to prevent moisture condensation cycles, with blown-fiberglass attic systems plus continuous soffit-and-ridge ventilation now the dominant Arkansas retrofit spec across both Ozark mountain and Delta lowland housing stock.

CA.name: California
CA.license_board_name: California Contractors State License Board — C-2 (Insulation & Acoustical)
CA.permit_typical: permit required for attic insulation retrofit (attached to Title 24 compliance documentation); CSLB C-2 license required above $500 contract; Title 24 Part 6 compliance mandatory on every retrofit
CA.distinctive_law: California Title 24 Part 6 Energy Code is the most restrictive residential energy code in the country — California requires R-49 attic minimum (R-38 floor for limited cases) plus mandatory blower-door testing, refrigerant charge verification, and HERS rater field verification on every residential insulation retrofit project, with the California Energy Commission's BUILD program additionally providing electrification rebates for IRA-funded HEAR upgrades and California Energy Commission Title 24 Part 11 (CALGreen) layering separate green building requirements on every covered residential project.
CA.climate_concern: California's dramatic climate variation from coastal Mediterranean (3C) to high desert (5B) drives 16 distinct California Energy Commission climate zones with separate Title 24 prescriptive R-value packages — coastal Northern California Bay Area focuses on heating-load minimization, while inland Central Valley and Southern California focus on attic-temperature reduction through radiant barrier sheathing and ventilation, and CAL FIRE Tier 3 WUI overlay across the Sierra foothills additionally drives Class A fire-rated mineral wool insulation specification in mapped fire-hazard zones.

CO.name: Colorado
CO.license_board_name: No statewide insulation license — Colorado Energy Office administers IRA HOMES + HEER; Denver, Colorado Springs, Aurora issue municipal contractor credentials
CO.permit_typical: permit not typical for attic insulation retrofit on existing residential; Denver Class C contractor registration for residential exterior contracting above $2,000
CO.distinctive_law: Colorado HB 22-1362 (Building Greenhouse Gas Emissions) requires every Colorado municipality with a building department to adopt the IECC 2021 model energy code or its equivalent by 2026 — Colorado is one of the first states to mandate IECC 2021 statewide, and Colorado HB 22-1218 separately created the Colorado Energy Office IRA HOMES + HEER administration that has been the fastest state to launch IRA-funded weatherization rebates in the Mountain West, with Xcel Energy and Black Hills Energy additionally co-funding utility insulation rebates statewide.
CO.climate_concern: Colorado's combination of 6,000 annual heating degree days, 36-inch frost depth on the Front Range, and high-altitude UV exposure across the Wasatch and Rocky Mountains drives heating-dominant insulation upgrades statewide — R-49 blown-cellulose attic plus R-20 wall cavity insulation plus continuous polyiso exterior on retrofits is now the dominant Colorado spec, with mountain-elevation Aspen, Vail, and Steamboat Springs additionally requiring R-60 attic and triple-pane glazing upgrades to manage extreme winter heating season costs.

CT.name: Connecticut
CT.license_board_name: Connecticut Department of Consumer Protection — Home Improvement Contractor (HIC) registration
CT.permit_typical: permit required for attic insulation retrofit; HIC registration mandatory above $200 contract
CT.distinctive_law: Connecticut PA 21-115 (Conservation & Load Management) created the most aggressive utility-funded residential weatherization program in the Northeast — Eversource and United Illuminating co-fund the Connecticut Home Energy Solutions program covering the majority of attic insulation upgrade cost on income-eligible Connecticut households, plus Connecticut became the first state to layer IRA-funded HEAR rebates on top of existing utility C&LM funding, doubling effective Connecticut residential insulation rebate amounts in 2024-2025.
CT.climate_concern: Connecticut's combination of 5,800 annual heating degree days, 42-inch frost depth, and the highest pre-1900 wood-frame housing stock per capita in New England creates the most expensive average insulation retrofit cycle in the Northeast — Hartford, New Haven, Bridgeport, and Stamford pre-1900 housing requires combination air-sealing + dense-pack cellulose wall cavity + blown-cellulose attic upgrades that typically run 30-50% higher per square foot than national norms, with knob-and-tube wiring removal additionally required on the majority of pre-1950 retrofits.

DE.name: Delaware
DE.license_board_name: Delaware Division of Revenue — Business License (insulation contractor)
DE.permit_typical: permit required for attic insulation retrofit in New Castle County; Delaware Business License only
DE.distinctive_law: Delaware Code Title 8 §8052 creates the Delaware Sustainable Energy Utility (DESEU) the only state-chartered nonprofit utility in the country dedicated exclusively to residential energy efficiency programs — DESEU administers the Delaware Energize program providing rebates on every attic insulation retrofit on income-eligible Delaware households, and DESEU is the planned IRA HOMES + HEER administrator pending state Department of Energy designation in 2026.
DE.climate_concern: Delaware's combination of 4,400 annual heating degree days, 1,300 cooling degree days, and Atlantic coastal humidity from Cape Henlopen to Bethany Beach creates a balanced heating-cooling insulation profile statewide — and Delaware Bay sandy fast-draining soils across Sussex County combined with high humidity drive radiant-barrier-plus-blown-fiberglass attic systems with continuous soffit-and-ridge ventilation on every coastal Delaware retrofit, plus closed-cell spray foam additionally specified on coastal homes addressing salt-air-driven attic moisture cycling.

FL.name: Florida
FL.license_board_name: Florida Department of Business & Professional Regulation — Construction Industry Licensing Board (Insulation Contractor)
FL.permit_typical: permit required for attic insulation retrofit per FBC Energy R503; FL DBPR insulation contractor license required statewide
FL.distinctive_law: Florida Building Code Energy Conservation §R503 imposes the most restrictive cooling-climate residential insulation rule in the country — every Florida residential insulation retrofit must achieve a Florida Energy Calculation tool (EnergyGauge) compliance score, with the Florida Building Code requiring duct-leakage testing on every retrofit that touches or modifies residential ductwork, and HVHZ Miami-Dade and Broward counties additionally requiring NOA-approved insulation product fastening schedules under FBC §1714 hurricane wind load requirements.
FL.climate_concern: Florida's combination of 4,400 annual cooling degree days, 75%+ year-round humidity, and zero meaningful heating load drives the most cooling-dominant insulation profile in the country — radiant-barrier sheathing plus R-38 blown-fiberglass attic systems are now the dominant Florida retrofit spec, with HVHZ Miami-Dade and Broward additionally requiring closed-cell spray foam at the roofline to manage hurricane-driven building-envelope flex plus humidity-driven attic moisture cycling on every Florida coastal home.

GA.name: Georgia
GA.license_board_name: Georgia State Licensing Board for Residential and General Contractors
GA.permit_typical: no permit required for attic insulation retrofit; residential-basic or residential-light contractor license required above $2,500 contract
GA.distinctive_law: Georgia OCGA §8-2-25 created the Georgia State Energy Code Amendment Process — every Georgia residential energy code update must go through the Georgia Department of Community Affairs State Codes Advisory Committee, plus separate review by the Construction Industry Licensing Board, the slowest amendment process in the Southeast and explaining why Georgia remains on IECC 2015 while neighboring Florida operates under a state-specific code and South Carolina has adopted IECC 2018.
GA.climate_concern: Georgia's mixed-humid climate from the Atlanta Piedmont to the Coastal Plain creates a cooling-leaning insulation profile with 2,900 heating and 2,300 cooling degree days — and Atlanta's red-clay subsoil plus high mature-deciduous tree canopy drives radiant-barrier-plus-blown-fiberglass attic systems with continuous soffit-and-ridge ventilation as the dominant Atlanta-Athens-Macon retrofit spec, with Savannah and Brunswick coastal homes additionally requiring closed-cell spray foam at the roofline to manage Gulf and Atlantic coastal humidity-driven attic moisture cycling.

HI.name: Hawaii
HI.license_board_name: Hawaii Department of Commerce & Consumer Affairs — Contractors License Board (C-32 specialty Insulation)
HI.permit_typical: permit required for attic insulation retrofit per Hawaii Energy Code; C-32 specialty license required statewide on contracts above $1,500
HI.distinctive_law: Hawaii HRS §107 imposes the Hawaii State Energy Code with separate prescriptive packages for each of the four major Hawaiian island county jurisdictions (Honolulu, Hawaii County, Maui County, Kauai County) — Hawaii is unusual in delegating final residential energy code adoption to county building departments under HRS §107-25, with Honolulu County typically operating under the most restrictive Honolulu Energy Conservation Code (Chapter 32) requiring above-IECC R-30 attic and triple-glazed window upgrades on every windward-facing exposure.
HI.climate_concern: Hawaii's island-tropical combination of 4,200 annual cooling degree days, zero meaningful heating load, and dramatic windward-vs-leeward microclimates drives radiant-barrier-plus-blown-fiberglass attic systems with continuous soffit-and-ridge ventilation as the dominant Hawaii retrofit spec — and persistent Pacific salt-spray on every windward-coastal exposure additionally drives closed-cell spray foam at the roofline plus marine-grade fastener replacement on Oahu, Maui, and Kauai windward residential properties to manage salt-air-driven attic moisture cycling.

ID.name: Idaho
ID.license_board_name: Idaho Division of Occupational and Professional Licenses — Contractor Registration
ID.permit_typical: permit not typical for attic insulation retrofit on existing residential; Idaho Contractor Registration required statewide
ID.distinctive_law: Idaho Code §39-4116 created the Idaho Building Code Board with statewide preemption authority over every municipal energy code amendment — Idaho explicitly blocks Boise, Moscow, Sun Valley, and Coeur d'Alene from adopting above-baseline residential energy codes, and Idaho remains on the IECC 2018 cycle pending the Idaho Building Code Board IECC 2024 review, leaving Idaho one of the slowest-adopting Mountain West states for residential energy code updates.
ID.climate_concern: Idaho's combination of 6,400 annual heating degree days, 36-inch frost depth, and high-altitude UV exposure across the Wood River Valley and Boise foothills drives heating-dominant insulation upgrades statewide — R-49 blown-fiberglass attic plus R-20 wall cavity insulation plus continuous polyiso exterior on retrofits is now the dominant Idaho spec, with mountain-elevation Sun Valley and McCall additionally requiring R-60 attic upgrades and triple-pane glazing on every premium ski-town residential retrofit to manage extreme winter heating costs above $400/month.

IL.name: Illinois
IL.license_board_name: No statewide insulation license — Illinois Energy Office administers IRA HOMES + HEER; Chicago Department of Buildings Class B contractor credentials
IL.permit_typical: permit required for attic insulation retrofit per Illinois Energy Conservation Code; Chicago Class B General Contractor registration required for any city residential repaint contract above $5,000
IL.distinctive_law: Illinois Energy Conservation Code (20 ILCS 3125) makes the Illinois Capital Development Board the statewide energy code authority — Illinois adopted IECC 2021 effective 2023 statewide with no local amendment authority, and the Climate and Equitable Jobs Act (CEJA, PA 102-0662) separately created the Illinois Solar for All program providing low-income residential weatherization plus rooftop solar bundled retrofits funded by Illinois Power Agency procurements.
IL.climate_concern: Illinois's combination of 5,600 annual heating degree days in the Chicagoland-Rockford-Peoria corridor, 42-inch frost depth, and the highest pre-1978 housing stock concentration in the Midwest creates the most expensive average insulation retrofit cycle in the Midwest — Chicago bungalow belt, Rockford, and Peoria pre-1978 housing requires combination air-sealing + dense-pack cellulose wall cavity + blown-cellulose attic upgrades that typically run 30-50% higher per square foot than national norms, with knob-and-tube wiring removal additionally required on the majority of pre-1950 retrofits.

IN.name: Indiana
IN.license_board_name: No statewide insulation license — Indianapolis, Fort Wayne, Evansville issue municipal contractor credentials
IN.permit_typical: no permit required for attic insulation retrofit on existing residential; Indianapolis Class A Improvement Contractor registration required for any city contract above $5,000
IN.distinctive_law: Indiana Code §22-12 grants the Indiana Department of Homeland Security Fire Prevention & Building Safety Commission statewide energy code authority — Indiana adopted IECC 2021 effective 2024 statewide and explicitly preempts every municipal energy code amendment, blocking Indianapolis, Fort Wayne, Evansville, and South Bend from adopting above-baseline residential energy codes, and the Indiana Office of Energy Development is the planned IRA HOMES + HEER administrator pending state energy office IRA implementation in 2026.
IN.climate_concern: Indiana's combination of 5,400 annual heating degree days across the Indianapolis-Fort Wayne-South Bend corridor, 36-inch frost depth, and the highest concentration of post-WWII subdivision Cape Cod and ranch housing stock in the Midwest creates standardized insulation retrofit demand statewide — blown-fiberglass attic upgrades from R-19 to R-49 are now the dominant Indiana retrofit spec, with combination wall cavity + air-sealing upgrades additionally specified on the majority of Indianapolis pre-1960 housing retrofits.

IA.name: Iowa
IA.license_board_name: Iowa Workforce Development — Construction Contractor Registration + Iowa State Building Code Bureau
IA.permit_typical: permit required for attic insulation retrofit per Iowa Energy Code; Iowa Construction Contractor Registration required statewide above $2,000 annual gross
IA.distinctive_law: Iowa Code Chapter 103A makes the Iowa State Building Code the statewide residential energy code basis — Iowa was one of the first Midwestern states to adopt IECC 2021 effective 2023 statewide, and the Iowa Economic Development Authority IRA HOMES program launched the fastest IRA rebate disbursement in the Midwest, with MidAmerican Energy and Alliant Energy additionally co-funding utility insulation rebates layered on top of IRA HOMES funding throughout 2024-2025.
IA.climate_concern: Iowa's combination of 6,300 annual heating degree days, -25°F winter low temperatures across the entire state, and 48-inch frost depth drives heating-dominant insulation retrofits statewide — R-49 blown-cellulose attic plus R-20 wall cavity insulation plus continuous polyiso exterior on retrofits is now the dominant Iowa spec, with mid-cycle wash-down of attic insulation following central-state corn-pollen surge in late July measurably extending retrofit-to-retrofit useful life on every Iowa attic install.

KS.name: Kansas
KS.license_board_name: No statewide insulation license; Wichita, Overland Park, Kansas City KS issue municipal contractor credentials
KS.permit_typical: permit not typical for attic insulation retrofit on existing residential; municipal contractor registration required in Wichita and Overland Park
KS.distinctive_law: Kansas Statute §75-1219 created the Kansas Energy Office under the Kansas Corporation Commission with limited authority to administer voluntary residential energy efficiency programs, but Kansas Statute §12-756 explicitly preempts every municipal residential energy code amendment, leaving Kansas one of the most fragmented residential energy code landscapes in the country with no statewide enforcement mechanism, and the Kansas Energy Office IRA HOMES + HEER program remains in planning pending Kansas Legislature implementation language in 2026.
KS.climate_concern: Kansas's combination of 4,900 annual heating degree days, 1,700 cooling degree days, and 36-inch frost depth creates a balanced heating-cooling insulation profile statewide — and central-plains hailstorms with 2-inch+ diameter routinely damage attic ridge vents and roof penetrations during the April-June severe weather season, driving combined roof-replacement-and-insulation-upgrade contracts on hail-prone Wichita, Topeka, and Garden City ZIP codes plus impact-rated ridge ventilation on every premium Kansas attic retrofit.

KY.name: Kentucky
KY.license_board_name: No statewide insulation license — Louisville Metro and Lexington-Fayette Urban County issue municipal contractor credentials
KY.permit_typical: no permit required for attic insulation retrofit on existing residential; Louisville Metro contractor registration required for residential exterior contracting above $5,000
KY.distinctive_law: Kentucky KRS §198B grants the Kentucky Department of Housing, Buildings and Construction statewide energy code authority — Kentucky operates under the Kentucky Residential Code 2019 (based on IECC 2018) and explicitly preempts every municipal energy code amendment, blocking Louisville, Lexington, and Bowling Green from adopting above-baseline residential energy codes, and the Kentucky Department for Energy Development & Independence is the planned IRA HOMES + HEER administrator pending state energy office IRA implementation in 2026.
KY.climate_concern: Kentucky's Bluegrass region combination of 4,500 annual heating degree days, 1,500 cooling degree days, and high mature-deciduous tree canopy in Louisville Old Louisville and Lexington Chevy Chase historic neighborhoods creates a balanced heating-cooling insulation profile — and Kentucky's brick-veneer-dominant residential stock shifts typical insulation scope toward attic-only retrofits on most homes built post-1975, with full-elevation wall cavity insulation upgrades concentrated in Eastern Kentucky Appalachian wood-lap housing stock plus pre-1950 Louisville and Lexington historic neighborhoods.

LA.name: Louisiana
LA.license_board_name: Louisiana State Licensing Board for Contractors — Residential Building Contractor or Specialty
LA.permit_typical: permit required for attic insulation retrofit per Louisiana State Uniform Construction Code; LSLBC residential license required above $7,500 contract
LA.distinctive_law: Louisiana RS 40:1722 makes the Louisiana State Uniform Construction Code Council the statewide residential energy code authority — Louisiana adopted IECC 2021 effective 2024 statewide, the only Gulf Coast state on the current IECC cycle, and the Louisiana Department of Natural Resources Energy Office is the IRA HOMES + HEER administrator with planned 2026 launch, plus Louisiana Energy Conservation Code (LSUCC §13-3) requires duct-leakage testing on every retrofit that touches or modifies residential ductwork, the most restrictive Gulf Coast duct-leakage rule.
LA.climate_concern: Louisiana's combination of 3,000 annual cooling degree days, 80%+ year-round humidity, and minimal heating load drives a cooling-dominant insulation profile statewide with hurricane-resilience layered on top — radiant-barrier-plus-blown-fiberglass attic systems plus closed-cell spray foam at the roofline are now the dominant Louisiana retrofit spec, with hurricane wind-zone homes additionally requiring NOA-approved insulation product fastening schedules and continuous Tyvek-wrapped wall cavity barriers to manage hurricane-driven building-envelope flex on every coastal Louisiana home.

ME.name: Maine
ME.license_board_name: No statewide insulation license; Efficiency Maine Trust administers IRA HOMES + HEER
ME.permit_typical: permit required for attic insulation retrofit per Maine Uniform Building & Energy Code (MUBEC); Efficiency Maine Trust Registered Insulation Contractor required for rebate-funded retrofits
ME.distinctive_law: Maine 25 MRS §2371 created the Maine Uniform Building & Energy Code (MUBEC) the most aggressive statewide residential energy code in New England — Maine adopted IECC 2021 with state-specific R-60 attic minimum (above the IECC R-49 baseline) and mandatory blower-door testing on every new residential construction, plus Efficiency Maine Trust administers the most generous state-funded weatherization program in New England covering up to 100% of attic and wall insulation upgrades for income-eligible Maine households layered with IRA-funded HEAR rebates.
ME.climate_concern: Maine's combination of 7,500 annual heating degree days, -30°F winter low temperatures across interior climate zones 6A-7, and 60-inch frost depth drives the most heating-dominant insulation profile in the Lower 48 — R-60 blown-cellulose attic plus R-20 wall cavity insulation plus continuous polyiso exterior on retrofits is now the dominant Maine spec, with coastal Maine Penobscot Bay, Casco Bay, and Mount Desert Island homes additionally requiring closed-cell spray foam at the roofline plus marine-grade fastener replacement to manage salt-air-driven attic moisture cycling.

MD.name: Maryland
MD.license_board_name: Maryland Home Improvement Commission (MHIC) — Home Improvement Contractor
MD.permit_typical: permit required for attic insulation retrofit per Maryland Building Performance Standards; MHIC license required above $500 contract
MD.distinctive_law: Maryland Building Performance Standards (Annotated Code §12-503) imposes the most restrictive statewide energy code in the Mid-Atlantic — Maryland adopted IECC 2021 with state-specific air-sealing requirements that exceed IECC baseline, plus Maryland HB 831 (2022) established the Maryland Energy Administration EmPOWER Maryland weatherization program providing rebates for attic insulation upgrades layered with IRA HOMES + HEER funding, doubling effective Maryland residential insulation rebate amounts in 2024-2025.
MD.climate_concern: Maryland's combination of 4,800 annual heating degree days, 1,300 cooling degree days, and Chesapeake Bay watershed humidity creates a balanced heating-cooling insulation profile — and Maryland's pre-1978 housing-stock concentration in Baltimore rowhouse districts triggers combination air-sealing + dense-pack cellulose wall cavity + blown-cellulose attic upgrades on the majority of urban Maryland insulation retrofit scope, with brick-veneer rowhouse design requiring specialty narrow-cavity dense-pack cellulose installation through interior wall penetrations.

MA.name: Massachusetts
MA.license_board_name: Massachusetts Office of Consumer Affairs — Home Improvement Contractor (HIC) registration
MA.permit_typical: permit required for attic insulation retrofit per Massachusetts Stretch Code or Specialized Energy Code; HIC registration required above $1,000 contract
MA.distinctive_law: Massachusetts 225 CMR 22 created the Massachusetts Stretch Code and Specialized Energy Code (the Net-Zero Stretch) — Massachusetts is the only state with a tiered energy code system where 300+ municipalities have adopted the Stretch Code (10-20% above IECC baseline) and 50+ have adopted the Specialized Energy Code requiring net-zero-ready new construction, and Mass Save administers the most generous utility-funded residential weatherization program in the country covering up to 100% of attic insulation upgrades on income-eligible Massachusetts households layered with IRA HEAR rebates.
MA.climate_concern: Massachusetts's combination of 5,800 annual heating degree days, 48-inch frost depth in interior climate zone 5A-6A, and the highest pre-1900 housing stock per capita in the country drives the most expensive average insulation retrofit cycle in the country — Boston, Cambridge, Worcester, and Springfield pre-1900 housing requires combination air-sealing + dense-pack cellulose wall cavity + R-60 blown-cellulose attic upgrades, with Mass Save Stretch Code-compliant retrofits typically running 30-50% higher per square foot than national norms but offset by Mass Save rebates.

MI.name: Michigan
MI.license_board_name: Michigan Department of Licensing and Regulatory Affairs — Maintenance & Alteration Contractor (Insulation specialty)
MI.permit_typical: permit required for attic insulation retrofit per Michigan Uniform Energy Code; Michigan M&A Contractor (Insulation) license required above $600 contract
MI.distinctive_law: Michigan PA 230 of 1972 imposes the Michigan Uniform Energy Code — Michigan remains on the IECC 2015 cycle (the slowest energy code basis in the Midwest after Indiana), and the Michigan Public Service Commission is the planned IRA HOMES + HEER administrator pending 2026 launch through the Michigan Energy Office, while Michigan's existing utility-funded Home Performance with ENERGY STAR program through DTE Energy and Consumers Energy provides residential insulation rebates layered with planned IRA HEAR funding.
MI.climate_concern: Michigan's combination of 6,700 annual heating degree days, Great Lakes lake-effect humidity from Lake Michigan and Lake Huron, and 42-inch frost depth drives heating-dominant insulation upgrades statewide — and Michigan's heavily-shaded suburban lots from Detroit Metro to Grand Rapids restrict attic ventilation on north-facing elevations, requiring careful ventilation balance with insulation upgrades to prevent moisture condensation cycles plus mid-cycle wash-down of attic insulation following Lake-effect snow melt seasons measurably extending retrofit-to-retrofit useful life on every Michigan attic install.

MN.name: Minnesota
MN.license_board_name: Minnesota Department of Labor & Industry — Residential Building Contractor or Specialty
MN.permit_typical: permit required for attic insulation retrofit per Minnesota State Energy Code; DLI residential contractor license required above $15,000 annual gross
MN.distinctive_law: Minnesota Statute §326B.106 imposes the Minnesota State Energy Code (Minnesota Rules Chapter 1322) with state-specific R-60 attic minimum (above the IECC R-49 baseline) plus mandatory blower-door testing on every new residential construction — and Minnesota was one of the first Midwestern states to adopt the IECC 2021 base code in 2024 with separate Cold-Climate Energy Code amendments, plus Center for Energy and Environment administers the most generous utility-funded weatherization program in the Midwest covering up to 100% of attic insulation upgrades on income-eligible Minnesota households layered with IRA-funded HEAR rebates.
MN.climate_concern: Minnesota's combination of 7,900 annual heating degree days, -40°F winter low temperatures across the entire state, and 60-inch frost depth drives the most heating-dominant insulation profile in the Lower 48 alongside Maine — R-60 blown-cellulose attic plus R-20 wall cavity insulation plus continuous polyiso exterior on retrofits is now the dominant Minnesota spec, with Iron Range and Boundary Waters region homes additionally requiring R-80 attic upgrades and triple-pane glazing on every premium retrofit to manage extreme winter heating costs above $500/month.

MS.name: Mississippi
MS.license_board_name: Mississippi State Board of Contractors — Residential Builder or Specialty
MS.permit_typical: no permit required for attic insulation retrofit on existing residential; MSBC residential license required above $50,000 contract or $10,000 in annual gross
MS.distinctive_law: Mississippi Code §17-2-1 grants Mississippi municipalities optional residential energy code adoption authority — Mississippi has no statewide residential energy code mandate (the only Gulf Coast state with no state-level energy code), leaving Jackson, Hattiesburg, and Gulf Coast municipalities to adopt voluntary IECC 2009 or higher at municipal discretion, the lowest residential energy code framework in the country and forcing voluntary above-code insulation upgrades through utility-incentive Entergy Mississippi and Mississippi Power programs rather than statewide code mandates.
MS.climate_concern: Mississippi's combination of 2,300 annual cooling degree days, 80%+ year-round humidity, and persistent Gulf Coast humidity from Bay St. Louis to Pascagoula drives a cooling-dominant insulation profile statewide — radiant-barrier-plus-blown-fiberglass attic systems plus closed-cell spray foam at the roofline are now the dominant Mississippi retrofit spec, with hurricane-zone homes additionally requiring NOA-approved insulation product fastening schedules and continuous Tyvek-wrapped wall cavity barriers on every coastal Mississippi home.

MO.name: Missouri
MO.license_board_name: No statewide insulation license — St. Louis, Kansas City MO, Springfield issue municipal contractor credentials
MO.permit_typical: permit not typical for attic insulation retrofit on existing residential; St. Louis Class B contractor registration required for residential exterior contracting above $5,000
MO.distinctive_law: Missouri Revised Statutes §640.150 grants Missouri municipalities optional residential energy code adoption authority — Missouri has no statewide residential energy code mandate, leaving St. Louis, Kansas City, Springfield, and Columbia to adopt voluntary IECC 2009 or higher at municipal discretion, plus Missouri Department of Natural Resources Division of Energy is the planned IRA HOMES + HEER administrator pending 2026 launch with Ameren Missouri and Spire utility insulation rebates currently providing the only statewide residential weatherization channel.
MO.climate_concern: Missouri's combination of 4,700 annual heating degree days, 1,500 cooling degree days, and 30-inch frost depth creates a balanced heating-cooling insulation profile statewide — and Missouri's transition zone between Northern cool-season and Southern warm-season climate creates dramatic seasonal attic-temperature swings that drive radiant-barrier-plus-blown-fiberglass attic systems plus continuous soffit-and-ridge ventilation as the dominant Missouri retrofit spec, with St. Louis Central West End and Kansas City pre-1950 brick-veneer housing additionally requiring narrow-cavity dense-pack cellulose installation.

MT.name: Montana
MT.license_board_name: Montana Department of Labor & Industry — Independent Contractor Registration
MT.permit_typical: permit required for attic insulation retrofit per Montana State Building Code; Montana Independent Contractor Registration required statewide
MT.distinctive_law: Montana Code §50-60 makes the Montana Department of Labor & Industry Building Codes Bureau the statewide residential energy code authority — Montana adopted IECC 2021 effective 2024 statewide, the most current energy code basis in the Mountain West alongside Colorado and Maine, and the Montana Department of Environmental Quality Energy Office is the planned IRA HOMES + HEER administrator with planned 2026 launch through the existing NorthWestern Energy E+ residential weatherization program providing utility-funded insulation rebates statewide.
MT.climate_concern: Montana's combination of 7,800 annual heating degree days, -40°F winter low temperatures across the entire state, and 48-inch frost depth drives heating-dominant insulation upgrades statewide — R-49 blown-fiberglass attic plus R-20 wall cavity insulation plus continuous polyiso exterior on retrofits is now the dominant Montana spec, with mountain-elevation Bozeman, Big Sky, and Whitefish additionally requiring R-60 attic upgrades and triple-pane glazing on every premium ski-town residential retrofit to manage extreme winter heating costs above $500/month.

NE.name: Nebraska
NE.license_board_name: Nebraska Department of Labor — Contractor Registration
NE.permit_typical: permit required for attic insulation retrofit per Nebraska State Energy Code; Nebraska Contractor Registration required statewide above $2,500 annual gross
NE.distinctive_law: Nebraska Revised Statute §81-1606 created the Nebraska State Energy Code statewide — Nebraska operates under IECC 2018 statewide with no municipal amendment authority, and the Nebraska Energy Office is the planned IRA HOMES + HEER administrator with planned 2026 launch through the existing Nebraska Public Power District and Omaha Public Power District residential weatherization programs providing utility-funded insulation rebates layered with planned IRA HEAR funding.
NE.climate_concern: Nebraska's combination of 6,100 annual heating degree days, 42-inch frost depth, and central-plains chinook wind temperature swings drives heating-dominant insulation upgrades statewide — R-49 blown-fiberglass attic plus R-20 wall cavity insulation plus continuous polyiso exterior on retrofits is now the dominant Nebraska spec, with central-plains hailstorms with 2-inch+ diameter routinely damaging attic ridge vents and roof penetrations during the April-June severe weather season driving combined roof-replacement-and-insulation-upgrade contracts on hail-prone Lincoln and Omaha ZIP codes.

NV.name: Nevada
NV.license_board_name: Nevada State Contractors Board — license C-37 (Insulation)
NV.permit_typical: permit required for attic insulation retrofit per Nevada Energy Conservation Code; NSCB C-37 specialty license required statewide on contracts above $1,000
NV.distinctive_law: Nevada NRS §701.220 created the Nevada State Office of Energy with statewide IRA HOMES + HEER program administration — Nevada was one of the first Western states to launch IRA-funded weatherization rebates through the Nevada Energy Assistance Program, and NV Energy additionally co-funds utility insulation rebates layered with IRA HEAR funding statewide, with the Southern Nevada Water Authority cross-subsidizing insulation upgrades alongside indoor water-conservation retrofits in Las Vegas Valley HUD income-eligible households.
NV.climate_concern: Nevada's combination of 3,000 annual heating degree days, 3,300 annual cooling degree days, and dramatic Las Vegas Valley vs Reno-Sparks climate variation creates a balanced heating-cooling insulation profile statewide — radiant-barrier-plus-blown-fiberglass attic systems are now the dominant Las Vegas Valley retrofit spec to manage 4,200 annual cooling degree days plus extreme summer attic temperatures, while Reno-Sparks and Carson City higher-altitude installs additionally require closed-cell spray foam at the roofline to manage the longer winter heating season.

NH.name: New Hampshire
NH.license_board_name: No statewide insulation license; New Hampshire Saves administers utility-funded weatherization rebates
NH.permit_typical: permit required for attic insulation retrofit per New Hampshire State Energy Code (RSA 155-D)
NH.distinctive_law: New Hampshire RSA 155-D imposes the New Hampshire State Energy Code statewide — New Hampshire operates under IECC 2018 with mandatory blower-door testing on every new residential construction, and NHSaves administers the utility-funded New Hampshire Home Performance with ENERGY STAR program through Eversource, Liberty Utilities, NH Electric Co-op, and Unitil providing residential insulation rebates that are the planned base layer for IRA HOMES + HEER funding pending New Hampshire Department of Energy IRA implementation in 2026.
NH.climate_concern: New Hampshire's combination of 7,100 annual heating degree days, -25°F winter low temperatures across interior climate zones 5A-6A, and 48-inch frost depth drives heating-dominant insulation upgrades statewide — and New Hampshire's high concentration of pre-1900 wood-frame housing stock in Manchester, Concord, Nashua, and Portsmouth requires combination air-sealing + dense-pack cellulose wall cavity + blown-cellulose attic upgrades on the majority of urban New Hampshire insulation retrofit scope, with knob-and-tube wiring removal additionally required on the majority of pre-1950 retrofits.

NJ.name: New Jersey
NJ.license_board_name: New Jersey Division of Consumer Affairs — Home Improvement Contractor (HIC) registration
NJ.permit_typical: permit required for attic insulation retrofit per New Jersey Uniform Construction Code; HIC registration required above $500 contract
NJ.distinctive_law: New Jersey N.J.S.A. 52:27D-119 imposes the New Jersey Uniform Construction Code with the New Jersey Energy Subcode (N.J.A.C. 5:23-3.18) — New Jersey adopted IECC 2021 effective 2023 statewide and was one of the first Northeast states to adopt the Stretch Code through New Jersey BPU's IRA HOMES + HEER program administration, plus New Jersey Clean Energy Program through Public Service Electric & Gas and Atlantic City Electric provides residential insulation rebates layered with IRA HEAR funding statewide.
NJ.climate_concern: New Jersey's combination of 5,000 annual heating degree days, 36-inch frost depth, and Atlantic coastal humidity from Cape May to Sandy Hook creates a balanced heating-cooling insulation profile statewide — and New Jersey's pre-1978 housing-stock concentration in Newark, Jersey City, Paterson, and Elizabeth combined with Jersey Shore coastal salt-spray drives combination air-sealing + dense-pack cellulose wall cavity + blown-cellulose attic upgrades on the majority of urban New Jersey insulation retrofit scope, with closed-cell spray foam additionally specified on every Jersey Shore coastal home.

NM.name: New Mexico
NM.license_board_name: New Mexico Regulation & Licensing Department — Construction Industries Division (GS-3 Insulation)
NM.permit_typical: permit required for attic insulation retrofit per New Mexico Energy Conservation Code; CID GS-3 specialty contractor license required statewide on contracts above $7,200
NM.distinctive_law: New Mexico NMSA §60-13-44 created the New Mexico Construction Industries Division GS-3 Insulation Contractor specialty license — and the New Mexico Energy, Minerals & Natural Resources Department is one of the IRA HOMES + HEER program early-adopters, providing rebates layered with PNM Power and El Paso Electric utility-funded weatherization programs, plus New Mexico's adobe and traditional Spanish Colonial Revival housing stock additionally qualifies for cultural-resource-preservation insulation grants through the New Mexico Historic Preservation Division on every pre-1955 historic residential retrofit.
NM.climate_concern: New Mexico's high-altitude combination of 4,400 annual heating degree days, intense Santa Fe and Taos UV exposure above 6,500 ft elevation, and dramatic diurnal temperature swings drive heating-dominant insulation upgrades on every Albuquerque, Santa Fe, and Taos retrofit — and Santa Fe and Taos historic-district adobe substrates require lime-amended interior insulation systems rather than standard blown cellulose to maintain vapor permeability, with Santa Fe HDRB historic-district review additionally requiring concealed insulation installation on every adobe substrate retrofit.

NY.name: New York
NY.license_board_name: No statewide insulation license — NYC Department of Consumer & Worker Protection HIC license; NYSERDA administers IRA HOMES + HEER
NY.permit_typical: permit required for attic insulation retrofit per New York State Energy Conservation Construction Code; NYC HIC license required above $200 contract within five boroughs
NY.distinctive_law: New York Energy Law §11-103 imposes the New York State Energy Conservation Construction Code (ECCCNYS) — New York adopted IECC 2021 effective 2024 statewide, plus the New York State Stretch Code (NYStretch) provides an opt-in 10-15% above-baseline pathway for municipalities, and NYSERDA administers the most generous state-funded weatherization program in the country through EmPower+ providing residential insulation rebates covering up to 100% of attic insulation upgrades on income-eligible New York households layered with IRA-funded HEAR rebates.
NY.climate_concern: New York's combination of 5,800 annual heating degree days in Upstate climate zones 5A-6A, Long Island Sound and Atlantic coastal salt-spray, and the highest pre-1900 housing-stock density in the country drives the most expensive average insulation retrofit cycle in the Northeast — NYC, Buffalo, Rochester, Syracuse, and Albany pre-1900 housing requires combination air-sealing + dense-pack cellulose wall cavity + R-49 blown-cellulose attic upgrades that typically run 30-60% higher per square foot than national norms, with knob-and-tube wiring removal additionally required on the majority of pre-1950 NYC and Upstate retrofits.

NC.name: North Carolina
NC.license_board_name: North Carolina Licensing Board for General Contractors (NCLBGC) — required above $40,000 contract; Insulation Specialty
NC.permit_typical: permit required for attic insulation retrofit per North Carolina Energy Conservation Code; NCLBGC General Contractor license required above $40,000 contract
NC.distinctive_law: North Carolina General Statutes §143-138 makes the North Carolina Building Code Council the statewide energy code authority — North Carolina remains on a state-modified IECC 2015 (with NC-specific amendments) due to extended Building Code Council review cycles, the slowest-adopting major Southeast state for residential energy code, and the North Carolina Department of Environmental Quality is the planned IRA HOMES + HEER administrator with planned 2026 launch through Duke Energy Progress and Duke Energy Carolinas residential weatherization programs.
NC.climate_concern: North Carolina's combination of 3,500 annual heating degree days, 1,900 cooling degree days, and dramatic east-west climate variation from the Outer Banks coastal humid (3A) to the Smoky Mountains (5A) drives state-specific insulation specifications — Charlotte, Raleigh, Greensboro, and Wilmington Coastal Plain installs focus on cooling-dominant attic systems, while Asheville, Boone, and Western Carolina mountain installs require heating-dominant attic systems with closed-cell spray foam at the roofline plus continuous polyiso exterior.

ND.name: North Dakota
ND.license_board_name: North Dakota Secretary of State — Contractor License
ND.permit_typical: permit required for attic insulation retrofit per North Dakota Building Code; ND Contractor License required statewide above $4,000 contract
ND.distinctive_law: North Dakota Century Code §54-21.3 makes the North Dakota Department of Commerce Division of Community Services the statewide energy code authority — North Dakota operates under IECC 2018 with state-specific cold-climate amendments, and the North Dakota Department of Commerce Energy Programs Office is the planned IRA HOMES + HEER administrator with planned 2026 launch through Otter Tail Power and Montana-Dakota Utilities residential weatherization programs providing utility-funded insulation rebates statewide.
ND.climate_concern: North Dakota's combination of 8,800 annual heating degree days, -45°F winter low temperatures across the entire state, and 60-inch frost depth drives the most heating-dominant insulation profile in the Lower 48 alongside Maine and Minnesota — R-49+ blown-fiberglass attic plus R-20 wall cavity insulation plus continuous polyiso exterior on retrofits is now the dominant North Dakota spec, with Bakken oil-patch fugitive dust contamination in the Williston, Tioga, and Watford City corridor measurably reducing attic insulation R-value over time and driving 12-15 year retrofit cycles versus 18-25 year national norms.

OH.name: Ohio
OH.license_board_name: No statewide insulation license — Cleveland, Columbus, Cincinnati issue municipal contractor credentials
OH.permit_typical: permit required for attic insulation retrofit per Ohio Residential Code; municipal contractor registration required in Cleveland, Cincinnati, Columbus on residential exterior contracting above $5,000
OH.distinctive_law: Ohio Revised Code §3781.10 makes the Ohio Board of Building Standards the statewide energy code authority — Ohio remains on the IECC 2009 cycle (the oldest energy code basis in the Midwest), and the Ohio Development Services Agency Energy Efficiency Office is the planned IRA HOMES + HEER administrator pending 2026 launch with American Electric Power Ohio, Duke Energy Ohio, and FirstEnergy Ohio utility-funded weatherization programs providing residential insulation rebates as the current statewide channel.
OH.climate_concern: Ohio's combination of 5,400 annual heating degree days, Lake Erie lake-effect humidity along the Cleveland-Toledo corridor, and the highest pre-1900 housing-stock density in the Midwest after Pennsylvania creates the most expensive average insulation retrofit cycle in the Midwest — Cleveland, Cincinnati, Columbus, Toledo, Youngstown, and Akron pre-1900 housing requires combination air-sealing + dense-pack cellulose wall cavity + blown-cellulose attic upgrades, with knob-and-tube wiring removal additionally required on the majority of pre-1950 Ohio retrofits.

OK.name: Oklahoma
OK.license_board_name: Oklahoma Construction Industries Board (Roofing & Painting registration via municipal channels)
OK.permit_typical: permit not typical for attic insulation retrofit on existing residential; Oklahoma City and Tulsa municipal contractor registration required for residential exterior contracting above $5,000
OK.distinctive_law: Oklahoma 59 OS §1000 limits the Oklahoma Construction Industries Board to plumbing, electrical, mechanical, and roofing trades only — leaving Oklahoma residential insulation contracting unregulated at the state level with no specialty insulation contractor license, and the Oklahoma Department of Commerce Office of Community Development is the planned IRA HOMES + HEER administrator pending 2026 launch with Oklahoma Gas & Electric and Public Service Co of Oklahoma utility-funded weatherization programs as the current statewide rebate channel.
OK.climate_concern: Oklahoma's combination of 3,700 annual heating degree days, 2,100 cooling degree days, and central-plains hailstorms with 2-inch+ diameter routinely damaging attic ridge vents and roof penetrations drives combined roof-replacement-and-insulation-upgrade contracts on hail-prone Oklahoma City, Tulsa, Edmond, and Norman ZIP codes — and Oklahoma's red-clay-derived dust contamination during high-wind spring events measurably reduces attic insulation R-value over time, driving 14-18 year retrofit cycles versus 18-25 year national norms on every Oklahoma attic install.

OR.name: Oregon
OR.license_board_name: Oregon Construction Contractors Board (CCB) — Residential General Contractor or Specialty
OR.permit_typical: permit required for attic insulation retrofit per Oregon Residential Specialty Code (Energy); CCB license required statewide on every contract regardless of value
OR.distinctive_law: Oregon ORS §455.500 imposes the Oregon Residential Specialty Code (Energy) the most restrictive Pacific Northwest residential energy code — Oregon adopted state-specific R-49 attic + R-21 wall + mandatory blower-door testing on every new residential construction, plus the Oregon Reach Code provides a separate stretch-code pathway for municipalities, and Energy Trust of Oregon administers utility-funded weatherization rebates layered with IRA-funded HEAR rebates through the Oregon Department of Energy IRA implementation.
OR.climate_concern: Oregon's Willamette Valley combination of 4,500 annual heating degree days, persistent Pacific Northwest mildew growing conditions, and Cascade Range wood-substrate moisture cycling drives heating-dominant insulation upgrades statewide — and Oregon Coast salt-spray on Astoria, Newport, Coos Bay, and Brookings homes additionally requires closed-cell spray foam at the roofline plus marine-grade fastener replacement on every coastal Oregon retrofit to manage salt-air-driven attic moisture cycling, with high-altitude Bend and Sisters additionally requiring R-60 attic upgrades on every premium residential install.

PA.name: Pennsylvania
PA.license_board_name: Pennsylvania Office of Attorney General — Home Improvement Contractor (HIC) registration
PA.permit_typical: permit required for attic insulation retrofit per Pennsylvania Uniform Construction Code; PA HIC registration required above $5,000 annual gross
PA.distinctive_law: Pennsylvania Act 45 of 1999 imposes the Pennsylvania Uniform Construction Code statewide — Pennsylvania remains on the IECC 2015 cycle through extended PA Department of Labor & Industry review, and the Pennsylvania Department of Environmental Protection Office of Energy & Climate is the planned IRA HOMES + HEER administrator pending 2026 launch with PECO Energy, PPL Electric Utilities, and Duquesne Light utility-funded weatherization programs providing residential insulation rebates as the current statewide channel.
PA.climate_concern: Pennsylvania's combination of 5,500 annual heating degree days in the Allegheny Plateau and Pocono Mountains, persistent Susquehanna and Delaware Valley humidity, and the highest pre-1900 housing-stock density in the Mid-Atlantic drives heating-dominant insulation upgrades statewide — Philadelphia, Pittsburgh, Allentown, and Erie pre-1900 housing requires combination air-sealing + dense-pack cellulose wall cavity + blown-cellulose attic upgrades, with knob-and-tube wiring removal additionally required on the majority of pre-1950 Pennsylvania retrofits.

RI.name: Rhode Island
RI.license_board_name: Rhode Island Contractors Registration & Licensing Board (CRLB)
RI.permit_typical: permit required for attic insulation retrofit per Rhode Island State Energy Code; CRLB Contractor Registration required statewide regardless of contract value
RI.distinctive_law: Rhode Island General Laws §23-27.3 imposes the Rhode Island State Energy Code (SBC-8) statewide — Rhode Island adopted IECC 2018 with state-specific air-sealing requirements that exceed IECC baseline, plus the Rhode Island Stretch Code provides an opt-in pathway for municipalities, and National Grid Rhode Island administers the most generous utility-funded weatherization program in New England covering up to 100% of attic insulation upgrades on income-eligible Rhode Island households layered with IRA-funded HEAR rebates through the Rhode Island Office of Energy Resources.
RI.climate_concern: Rhode Island's combination of 5,500 annual heating degree days, North Atlantic salt-spray on every Narragansett Bay and South County coastal exposure, and the highest pre-1900 housing-stock density per capita in the country drives the most expensive average insulation retrofit cycle in the smallest state — Providence, Pawtucket, Cranston, and Newport pre-1900 housing requires combination air-sealing + dense-pack cellulose wall cavity + blown-cellulose attic upgrades that typically run 30-50% higher per square foot than national norms, with closed-cell spray foam additionally specified on every Newport coastal home.

SC.name: South Carolina
SC.license_board_name: South Carolina Department of Labor, Licensing and Regulation — Residential Specialty
SC.permit_typical: permit required for attic insulation retrofit per South Carolina Energy Conservation Code; SC LLR residential license required above $5,000 contract
SC.distinctive_law: South Carolina Code §6-9-50 makes the South Carolina Building Codes Council the statewide energy code authority — South Carolina adopted IECC 2018 effective 2020 statewide, and the South Carolina Energy Office is the planned IRA HOMES + HEER administrator pending 2026 launch with Duke Energy Progress and Dominion Energy South Carolina residential weatherization programs providing the current statewide rebate channel for residential insulation upgrades.
SC.climate_concern: South Carolina's combination of 2,700 annual heating degree days, 2,100 cooling degree days, and persistent Atlantic salt-spray on the Lowcountry from Charleston to Hilton Head creates a balanced heating-cooling insulation profile — and South Carolina's hurricane-zone homes additionally require NOA-approved insulation product fastening schedules and continuous Tyvek-wrapped wall cavity barriers on every coastal South Carolina home, with Charleston historic-district insulation retrofits restricted to concealed installation methods preserving original masonry exterior.

SD.name: South Dakota
SD.license_board_name: No statewide insulation license; Sioux Falls, Rapid City issue municipal contractor credentials
SD.permit_typical: permit not typical for attic insulation retrofit on existing residential; municipal contractor registration required in Sioux Falls and Rapid City
SD.distinctive_law: South Dakota Codified Laws §11-10 grants South Dakota municipalities optional residential energy code adoption authority — South Dakota has no statewide residential energy code mandate, and the South Dakota Office of Energy Programs is the planned IRA HOMES + HEER administrator pending 2026 launch with Black Hills Energy and NorthWestern Energy residential weatherization programs providing the current statewide rebate channel for residential insulation upgrades.
SD.climate_concern: South Dakota's combination of 7,500 annual heating degree days, -35°F winter low temperatures across the entire state, and 60-inch frost depth drives heating-dominant insulation upgrades statewide — R-49 blown-fiberglass attic plus R-20 wall cavity insulation plus continuous polyiso exterior on retrofits is now the dominant South Dakota spec, with Black Hills high-altitude installs above 4,000 ft elevation in Spearfish and Lead additionally requiring R-60 attic upgrades on every premium residential install to manage extreme winter heating costs.

TN.name: Tennessee
TN.license_board_name: Tennessee Board for Licensing Contractors — required above $25,000 contract
TN.permit_typical: permit required for attic insulation retrofit per Tennessee State Building Code; TN BLC contractor license required above $25,000 contract; Home Improvement License above $3,000 in 6 specific counties
TN.distinctive_law: Tennessee Code §13-4-201 makes the Tennessee State Fire Marshal's Office the statewide residential energy code authority — Tennessee adopted IECC 2018 effective 2018 statewide with no municipal amendment authority, and the Tennessee Department of Environment & Conservation Office of Energy Programs is the planned IRA HOMES + HEER administrator pending 2026 launch with TVA EnergyRight residential weatherization program providing the current statewide rebate channel for residential insulation upgrades.
TN.climate_concern: Tennessee's combination of 3,700 annual heating degree days, 1,700 cooling degree days, and intense Cumberland Plateau and Smoky Mountain humidity creates a balanced heating-cooling insulation profile — and Tennessee's brick-veneer-dominant residential stock since the 1970s shifts typical insulation scope toward attic-only retrofits on most homes built post-1975, with full-elevation wall cavity insulation upgrades concentrated in Eastern Tennessee Appalachian wood-lap housing stock plus pre-1950 Memphis, Nashville, Chattanooga, and Knoxville historic neighborhoods.

TX.name: Texas
TX.license_board_name: No statewide insulation license; State Energy Conservation Office (SECO) administers IRA HOMES + HEER
TX.permit_typical: permit required for attic insulation retrofit per Texas Energy Conservation Code; municipal contractor registration required in Houston, Dallas, San Antonio, Austin, Fort Worth on residential exterior contracting
TX.distinctive_law: Texas Government Code §447.004 imposes the Texas Energy Conservation Code (IECC 2015 base with state amendments) statewide — Texas was the last major state to adopt residential energy code statewide, and the Texas State Energy Conservation Office (SECO) under the Comptroller is the IRA HOMES + HEER administrator with one of the largest IRA rebate allocations in the country at $689 million combined HOMES + HEER, providing residential insulation rebates layered with utility-funded Oncor, CenterPoint Energy, and CPS Energy weatherization programs.
TX.climate_concern: Texas's combination of 3,000 annual cooling degree days, 75%+ humidity in East Texas, and dramatic regional climate variation from Houston Gulf coastal humid (1A) to El Paso arid (4B) drives state-specific insulation specifications — Houston, Dallas, San Antonio, and Austin support cooling-dominant attic systems with radiant-barrier sheathing, while West Texas El Paso, Lubbock, and Amarillo additionally require closed-cell spray foam at the roofline to manage extreme summer attic temperatures above 160°F and the longer winter heating season.

UT.name: Utah
UT.license_board_name: Utah Division of Occupational and Professional Licensing — S350 (Insulation Contractor)
UT.permit_typical: permit required for attic insulation retrofit per Utah Energy Conservation Code; DOPL S350 Insulation Contractor license required statewide on contracts above $3,000
UT.distinctive_law: Utah Code §15A-3-201 imposes the Utah Energy Conservation Code statewide — Utah adopted IECC 2021 effective 2024 statewide alongside Colorado as the most current Mountain West energy code basis, and the Utah Division of Energy Development is one of the IRA HOMES + HEER program early-adopters providing rebates layered with Rocky Mountain Power and Dominion Energy Utah utility-funded weatherization programs covering attic insulation upgrades statewide.
UT.climate_concern: Utah's combination of 5,500 annual heating degree days, intense Salt Lake City and Provo high-altitude UV exposure above 4,500 ft elevation, and Wasatch Front winter inversion drives heating-dominant insulation upgrades statewide — R-49 blown-fiberglass attic plus R-20 wall cavity insulation plus continuous polyiso exterior on retrofits is now the dominant Utah spec, with Park City and Deer Valley high-altitude installs above 7,000 ft additionally requiring R-60 attic upgrades and triple-pane glazing on every premium ski-town residential retrofit.

VT.name: Vermont
VT.license_board_name: Vermont Office of Professional Regulation — Residential Contractor Registration
VT.permit_typical: permit required for attic insulation retrofit per Vermont Residential Building Energy Standards (RBES); Vermont Residential Contractor Registration required above $10,000 contract
VT.distinctive_law: Vermont 30 V.S.A. §51 imposes the Vermont Residential Building Energy Standards (RBES) — Vermont operates the most aggressive statewide residential energy code in New England with state-specific R-60 attic minimum (above the IECC R-49 baseline) plus mandatory blower-door testing on every new residential construction, and Efficiency Vermont administers the most generous utility-funded weatherization program in the country covering up to 100% of attic insulation upgrades on income-eligible Vermont households layered with IRA-funded HEAR rebates.
VT.climate_concern: Vermont's combination of 7,300 annual heating degree days, -30°F winter low temperatures across the entire state, and 60-inch frost depth drives the most heating-dominant insulation profile in New England alongside Maine — R-60 blown-cellulose attic plus R-20 wall cavity insulation plus continuous polyiso exterior on retrofits is now the dominant Vermont spec, with Northeast Kingdom and Green Mountains homes additionally requiring R-80 attic upgrades and triple-pane glazing on every premium retrofit to manage extreme winter heating costs.

VA.name: Virginia
VA.license_board_name: Virginia Department of Professional and Occupational Regulation — Class C Contractor (Insulation Specialty)
VA.permit_typical: permit required for attic insulation retrofit per Virginia Residential Code; DPOR Class C Contractor license required above $1,000 contract or $10,000 annual gross
VA.distinctive_law: Virginia Code §36-99 imposes the Virginia Uniform Statewide Building Code with the Virginia Energy Conservation Code subcode — Virginia adopted IECC 2018 with state-specific amendments through the Virginia Board of Housing and Community Development, and the Virginia Department of Energy is the planned IRA HOMES + HEER administrator pending 2026 launch with Dominion Energy Virginia and Appalachian Power Virginia utility-funded weatherization programs providing the current statewide rebate channel for residential insulation upgrades.
VA.climate_concern: Virginia's combination of 4,400 annual heating degree days, 1,500 cooling degree days, and Tidewater coastal humidity creates a balanced heating-cooling insulation profile statewide — and Virginia's brick-veneer-dominant residential stock since the 1980s shifts typical insulation scope toward attic-only retrofits on most homes built post-1985, with full-elevation wall cavity insulation upgrades concentrated in Northern Virginia I-66 corridor pre-1980 housing plus Tidewater pre-1950 historic neighborhoods in Norfolk, Portsmouth, and Newport News.

WA.name: Washington
WA.license_board_name: Washington State Department of Labor & Industries — Contractor Registration
WA.permit_typical: permit required for attic insulation retrofit per Washington State Energy Code (WSEC); L&I Contractor Registration required statewide regardless of contract value
WA.distinctive_law: Washington RCW §19.27A imposes the Washington State Energy Code (WSEC) the most aggressive statewide residential energy code in the Pacific Northwest — Washington requires state-specific R-60 attic + R-21 wall + mandatory blower-door testing on every new residential construction, plus the Washington State Building Code Council adopted the 2024 WSEC requiring electric-ready hot water heating + electric-ready space heating on every new residential construction, and the Washington State Energy Office administers IRA HOMES + HEER funding layered with Puget Sound Energy, Seattle City Light, and Tacoma Power utility weatherization rebates.
WA.climate_concern: Washington's Puget Sound combination of 5,300 annual heating degree days, persistent Pacific Northwest mildew growing conditions, and Cascade Range wood-substrate moisture cycling drives heating-dominant insulation upgrades statewide — R-60 blown-cellulose attic plus R-21 wall cavity insulation plus continuous polyiso exterior on retrofits is now the dominant Washington spec, with Washington Coast salt-spray homes additionally requiring closed-cell spray foam at the roofline plus marine-grade fastener replacement on every coastal Washington retrofit.

WV.name: West Virginia
WV.license_board_name: West Virginia Division of Labor — Contractor Licensing Board
WV.permit_typical: permit required for attic insulation retrofit per West Virginia State Building Code; WV Division of Labor Contractor License required above $2,500 contract
WV.distinctive_law: West Virginia Code §29-3B imposes the West Virginia State Building Code through the State Fire Commission — West Virginia remains on the IECC 2015 cycle through extended State Fire Commission review, and the West Virginia Office of Energy is the planned IRA HOMES + HEER administrator pending 2026 launch with Appalachian Power West Virginia and Mon Power residential weatherization programs providing the current statewide rebate channel for residential insulation upgrades.
WV.climate_concern: West Virginia's Appalachian mountain combination of 5,100 annual heating degree days, persistent Allegheny Plateau humidity, and high concentration of pre-1900 wood-frame housing stock drives heating-dominant insulation upgrades — Charleston, Huntington, Wheeling, and Morgantown pre-1900 housing requires combination air-sealing + dense-pack cellulose wall cavity + blown-cellulose attic upgrades, with knob-and-tube wiring removal additionally required on the majority of pre-1950 West Virginia retrofits in mountainous Appalachian housing stock.

WI.name: Wisconsin
WI.license_board_name: Wisconsin Department of Safety & Professional Services — Dwelling Contractor Certification
WI.permit_typical: permit required for attic insulation retrofit per Wisconsin Uniform Dwelling Code; WI Dwelling Contractor Certification required for any one- and two-family dwelling exterior contracting
WI.distinctive_law: Wisconsin Statute §101.63 imposes the Wisconsin Uniform Dwelling Code (UDC) statewide — Wisconsin remains on the IECC 2009 cycle through extended Wisconsin Department of Safety & Professional Services review (the oldest Midwest energy code basis tied with Ohio), and Focus on Energy administers the most generous utility-funded weatherization program in the Midwest providing residential insulation rebates layered with planned IRA HOMES + HEER funding through the Wisconsin Department of Administration Public Service Commission of Wisconsin coordination.
WI.climate_concern: Wisconsin's combination of 7,000 annual heating degree days, -25°F winter low temperatures across the entire state, and Lake Michigan and Lake Superior lake-effect humidity drives heating-dominant insulation upgrades statewide — R-49 blown-cellulose attic plus R-20 wall cavity insulation plus continuous polyiso exterior on retrofits is now the dominant Wisconsin spec, with Door County and Northwoods homes additionally requiring R-60 attic upgrades and triple-pane glazing on every premium residential install to manage extreme winter heating costs.

WY.name: Wyoming
WY.license_board_name: No statewide insulation license; Cheyenne, Casper, Jackson issue municipal contractor credentials
WY.permit_typical: permit required for attic insulation retrofit per Wyoming State Building Code; municipal contractor registration required in Cheyenne and Jackson
WY.distinctive_law: Wyoming Statute §35-9 grants Wyoming municipalities optional residential energy code adoption authority — Wyoming has no statewide residential energy code mandate (one of only a handful of states with no state energy code), leaving Cheyenne, Casper, Jackson, and Laramie to adopt voluntary IECC 2018 or higher at municipal discretion, and the Wyoming Energy Authority is the planned IRA HOMES + HEER administrator pending 2026 launch with Rocky Mountain Power Wyoming and Black Hills Energy Wyoming residential weatherization programs.
WY.climate_concern: Wyoming's high-altitude combination of 8,000 annual heating degree days, the highest-elevation residential development in the continental U.S. across Jackson Hole and Cody at 6,000+ ft elevation, and 48-inch frost depth drives heating-dominant insulation upgrades statewide — R-49 blown-fiberglass attic plus R-20 wall cavity insulation plus continuous polyiso exterior on retrofits is now the dominant Wyoming spec, with Jackson and Teton Village ski-town residential properties additionally requiring R-60 attic upgrades and triple-pane glazing on every premium retrofit to manage extreme winter heating costs.

DC.name: District of Columbia
DC.license_board_name: DC Department of Licensing & Consumer Protection — Home Improvement Contractor (HIC) license
DC.permit_typical: permit required for attic insulation retrofit per DC Energy Conservation Code; DC HIC license required above $300 contract
DC.distinctive_law: District of Columbia Energy Conservation Code (12-I DCMR) imposes the most aggressive municipal-jurisdiction residential energy code in the country — DC adopted IECC 2021 with the DC Net-Zero Stretch Code requiring net-zero-ready residential construction effective 2026, and DC Sustainable Energy Utility administers IRA HOMES + HEER funding layered with Pepco utility weatherization rebates providing residential insulation upgrade rebates that cover up to 100% of attic insulation upgrades on income-eligible DC households.
DC.climate_concern: District of Columbia's combination of 4,200 annual heating degree days, 1,500 cooling degree days, and Chesapeake Bay watershed humidity creates a balanced heating-cooling insulation profile — and DC's high concentration of pre-1900 brick rowhouse stock across Capitol Hill, Georgetown, Petworth, and Shaw requires combination air-sealing + dense-pack cellulose wall cavity + blown-cellulose attic upgrades through interior wall penetrations on the majority of DC residential insulation retrofit scope, with knob-and-tube wiring removal additionally required on the majority of pre-1950 DC retrofits.
